evidence: He is the chief executive officer and co-founder of Google DeepMind and Isomorphic Labs, and a UK Government AI Adviser

source.name: Demis Hassabis
source.notes_excerpt: Demis Hassabis is the CEO and co-founder of Google DeepMind, an artificial intelligence company that was founded in London in 2010 and acquired by Google in 2014. He shared the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry with John M. Jumper for their work on AlphaFold, an AI tool that predicts protein structures. He is also the founder and CEO of Isomorphic Labs, a biotechnology company focused on drug discover

target.name: UK government's Frontier AI Taskforce
target.notes_excerpt: UK government body established April 2023 with £100m mandate to evaluate frontier AI safety risks ahead of the Bletchley Park AI Safety Summit. Chaired by Ian Hogarth, it transitioned in November 2023 into the AI Safety Institute (later renamed AI Security Institute in 2025), which is a separate entity in this database.

evidence: Ben Reinhardt - Federation of American Scientists

source.name: Ben Reinhardt
source.notes_excerpt: Ben Reinhardt is the founder of Speculative Technologies, a nonprofit research organization working to turn science fiction into reality. He previously worked as an Entrepreneur-in-Residence at Entrepreneur First in Singapore, where he helped people build deep tech companies from scratch. His background includes work at NASA, Magic Leap, venture capital, and starting a robotics company.

target.name: Federation of American Scientists
target.notes_excerpt: DC-based science and technology policy nonprofit founded in 1945. FAS runs the Day One Project to translate scientific expertise into federal policy; active in AI governance, advocating for responsible AI innovation, NIST AISI funding, and federal AI adoption. Submitted AI Action Plan recommendations to the White House in 2025.

evidence: said his adviser, Arvind Narayanan, professor of computer science

source.name: Arvind Narayanan
source.notes_excerpt: Arvind Narayanan is a professor of computer science at Princeton University and the director of the Center for Information Technology Policy. He is a co-author of the book AI Snake Oil and the essay AI as Normal Technology, and a newsletter of the same name which is read by over 60,000 researchers, policy makers, journalists, and AI enthusiasts. target.name: Sayash Kapoor
target.notes_excerpt: Sayash Kapoor is a Ph.D. candidate in computer science at Princeton University's Center for Information Technology Policy. He is co-author with Arvind Narayanan of the book "AI Snake Oil: What Artificial Intelligence Can Do, What It Can't, and How to Tell the Difference". He was named among TIME's 100 most influential people in AI. He won the Porter Ogden Jacobus Fellowship, Princeton's top distin

evidence: Ph.D. in ML in the International Max-Planck Research Program (IMPRS) under the supervision of Philipp Hennig

source.name: Philipp Hennig
source.notes_excerpt: Chair for Methods of Machine Learning at University of Tübingen. Co-director of Tübingen AI Center since July 2025. Pioneer of probabilistic numerics — treating numerical computation as machine learning inference. Author of 'Probabilistic Numerics: Computation as Machine Learning' (Cambridge University Press, 2022). Previously at Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems. Organizer of ProbNum c

target.name: Marius Hobbhahn
target.notes_excerpt: Marius Hobbhahn is the CEO and co-founder of Apollo Research, a nonprofit AI safety research organization that focuses on understanding AI deception and scheming behavior. In 2025, he was named to TIME100 in AI 2025. Apollo Research has collaborated with major AI companies including OpenAI to reduce scheming in models like o3, and works with government bodies like the U.K.'s AI Safety Institute. H

evidence: I received my PhD from UC Berkeley where I was advised by Dawn Song and Jacob Steinhardt

source.name: Jacob Steinhardt
source.notes_excerpt: Co-authored influential "Concrete Problems in AI Safety" paper (2016). Founded Transluce in 2023 to create open-source tools for auditing AI systems and conducting public safety evaluations. His AI2050 project focuses on using LLMs to investigate and explain behavior of frontier models to detect failure modes before deployment. Considers himself a "worried optimist" about AI's societal impact.

target.name: Dan Hendrycks
target.notes_excerpt: Dan Hendrycks (born 1994 or 1995) is an American machine learning researcher who serves as the director of the Center for AI Safety, a nonprofit research organization based in San Francisco, California. He received a B.S. from the University of Chicago in 2018 and a Ph.D. from the University of California, Berkeley in Computer Science in 2022. He is the main author of the research paper that intro

target.name: U.S. Department of Labor
target.notes_excerpt: Federal cabinet department overseeing labor standards, workforce, and employment. Partnered with NSF on AI-ready workforce initiative (MOU for AI literacy, training pathways, labor market research). Hosts AI page at dol.gov/ai. She was employed at Google for 13 years, where she founded Google's Open Research group and co-founded the M-Lab. In 2018, she was a core

target.name: Lina Khan
target.notes_excerpt: Led FTC's AI enforcement including OpenAI investigation (July 2023), Rite Aid AI facial recognition ban (December 2023), and joint interagency AI statement with DOJ/CFPB/EEOC (April 2023). Published op-ed 'We Must Regulate A.I.' in New York Times. Estimated 15% p(doom) according to sources. source.notes_excerpt: Lawrence Lessig is the Roy L. Furman Professor of Law and Leadership at Harvard Law School. He is the founder of Equal Citizens and a founding board member of Creative Commons. He clerked for Judge Richard Posner on the 7th Circuit Court of Appeals and Justice Antonin Scalia on the United States Supreme Court. target.name: Tim Wu
target.notes_excerpt: Tim Wu is the Julius Silver Professor of Law, Science and Technology at Columbia Law School. He served as Special Assistant to President Biden for competition and tech policy until 2023. Wu coined the term 'net neutrality' and is known for his work on antitrust and competition policy. He authored the book 'The Age of Extraction: How Tech Platforms Conquered the Economy and Threaten Our Future Pros

source.notes_excerpt: Australian-American roboticist, MIT Professor Emeritus (Panasonic Professor of Robotics), former director of MIT CSAIL. target.notes_excerpt: Lynne Parker is Principal Deputy Director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy and Executive Director of the President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology. In January 2025, she was appointed by President Donald Trump as Executive Director of the President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology and Counselor to the Director of the White House Office of Sci

role: Science and Technology Advisor to the President
evidence: From October 3, 2022 to January 20, 2025, she served as the 12th director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy and the Science and Technology Advisor to the President

source.name: Arati Prabhakar
source.notes_excerpt: Arati Prabhakar served as the 12th director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy from October 3, 2022 to January 20, 2025, and was the Science and Technology Advisor to President Biden. She was the first woman and person of color to be confirmed by the U.S. Senate as the Director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy. She was instrumental in passing Presi

target.name: Joe Biden
target.notes_excerpt: 46th U.S. President (2021-2025). Issued Executive Order 14110 in October 2023, the most comprehensive U.S. AI governance action to that point, requiring safety reporting from frontier AI developers and coordinating federal agency standards. Also released the Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights in 2022.

evidence: It authorizes the U.S. Artificial Intelligence Safety Institute (AISI) at the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)

source.name: Senate Commerce, Science, and Transportation Committee
source.notes_excerpt: The Senate Commerce, Science, and Transportation Committee is a standing committee of the United States Senate composed of 28 Senators and led by Committee Chairman Ted Cruz (R-Texas) and Ranking Member Maria Cantwell (D-Wash.). The committee has broad jurisdiction over all matters concerning interstate commerce, science and technology policy, and transportation, and is one of the largest of the S

target.name: National Institute of Standards and Technology
target.notes_excerpt: NIST develops voluntary AI standards and risk management frameworks for federal agencies and industry. Its AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0) is widely adopted. In 2025-26 its AI Safety Institute was renamed the Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) under the Trump administration's AI Action Plan, with a mandate to revise the AI RMF.

evidence: Part of the SCI Institute is the One-U Responsible Artificial Intelligence Initiative (One-U RAI), which aims to advance AI to achieve societal good, all while protecting privacy, civil rights, and civil liberties, and promoting fairness, accountability, and transparency.

source.name: Responsible AI Initiative
source.notes_excerpt: Multiple organizations operate under the name "Responsible AI Initiative" with different scopes and affiliations. The University of Utah operates the One-U Responsible AI Initiative, which aims to advance AI to achieve societal good while protecting privacy, civil rights, and civil liberties. Emory University launched a Responsible AI website as part of their enterprise-wide Responsible AI initiat

target.name: University of Utah
target.notes_excerpt: University of Utah hosts the One-U Responsible AI Initiative, a $100M transdisciplinary program led by the Scientific Computing and Imaging (SCI) Institute. A $50M AI supercomputer partnership with NVIDIA and HPE is slated to come online in summer 2026. Research focuses on healthcare, societal wellness, and public services.

evidence: The site represents a major milestone in the university's enterprise-wide Responsible AI initiative, a multi‑year effort led by the Emory AI Group with input from the Responsible AI Executive Governance Committee and partners across Emory University and Emory Healthcare.

target.name: Block Center for Technology and Society
target.notes_excerpt: The Block Center for Technology and Society at Carnegie Mellon University was established in 2019 to study how emerging technologies alter the future of work, how AI and analytics can be harnessed for social good, and how innovation can reduce inequality.
